cataloging_subject.label: Generydes (stanzaic version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English stanzaic version of Generydes, represented principally by Trinity College Cambridge MS O.5.2, ff. 1r–37v, and fragmentarily by Emmanuel College MS 405 and early printed witnesses. It is not the independent 10,086-line couplet version in Morgan MS M.876, the whole composite Trinity codex, William Aldis Wright's two-part 1873–1878 edition as a publication carrier, Wright's comparison appendix, the University of Michigan encoding and linked page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: Generydes (stanzaic version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed in the late fourteenth century; attested in late-fifteenth-century manuscripts; first known print c. 1504

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from the edition spelling and modern version-level bibliography

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this seven-line rhyme-royal text from the independent couplet version.

titles.value: Generydes, a Romance in Seven-Line Stanzas

assertion.notes: This is an edition title, not a manuscript rubric. The same title page inaccurately calls O.5.2 paper and dates it about 1440; current Trinity cataloging identifies parchment and the modern romance database dates the witness c. 1470.

titles.value: In olde Romans and storys as I rede, Of Inde Somtyme ther was a nobyll kyng
Explicit the boke of Generydes and of his faire lady Clarionas

assertion.notes: Wright says both authorship and source remain obscure. The inherited U-M AUTHOR field names Wright because he edited the source volume; it is not medieval authorship evidence.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: Wright inferred that the story was translated twice into English but found no French source. The distinct couplet version's prologue claims French and Latin intermediaries; that claim is not transferred to this stanzaic version as a resolved source chain.

scope.description: The 1873–1878 two-part edition and comparison appendix represented in the encoding

notes: Wright usually printed O.5.2 as written, corrected some obvious sense-destroying errors, retained manuscript readings in footnotes or the glossary, and did not regularize every defective line.

assertion.notes: This is the modern romance database's work-level date, not the date of either surviving manuscript or Wright's edition.

date_statements.display: Attested in Trinity College MS O.5.2, c. 1470, and fragmentarily in Emmanuel College MS 405, last third of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The production ranges overlap and current evidence does not establish which witness was copied first, so no single source_lineage_entity_id is assigned.

date_statements.display: First known printed edition c. 1504, surviving in a three-leaf Richard Pynson fragment

assertion.notes: Only a fragment survives; this does not prove that the original printed edition was itself fragmentary. Wright's complete scholarly edition was issued separately as EETS Original Series 55 and 70 in 1873 and 1878.
The modern romance database assigns the broader romance to the Midlands. This claim is kept distinct from O.5.2's copying and ownership history and from its current Cambridge location.
Modern English title-page wording, notes, and comparison apparatus belong to the edition layer and do not make the medieval romance multilingual.

form_statements.description: A Middle English narrative poem in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas, normally ababbcc in pentameter. The encoding has 1,000 LG containers and 6,995 L elements; two LG containers are truncated around the 187-line physical lacuna after line 4619.

subject_statements.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy

summaries.value: Generydes, the unacknowledged son of King Auferius of India and Sereyne of Syria, enters his father's service and is driven away after rejecting Queen Serenydes and being attacked by the traitor Amelok. In Persia he wins military renown and the love of Clarionas, defeats rival kings, rescues her in disguise, helps restore his father's kingdom, survives further separations and betrayals, and ultimately marries Clarionas and succeeds to rule. The romance joins dynastic restoration, courtly love, battlefield prowess, disguise and recognition, loyal friendship, and providential prediction.

This record describes only the stanzaic version. The independent couplet version in Morgan MS M.876 has a different 10,086-line text and source prologue. O.5.2 is the principal complete witness; the nine damaged Emmanuel folios and three early printed editions survive fragmentarily and do not become sources of Wright's continuous text. Wright's printed comparison appendix is encoded but remains modern apparatus outside the romance.

The romance has 6,995 extant edited and encoded lines in 1,000 XML LG containers. Of those containers, 998 have seven lines, one has six lines, and one has three lines; the two short groups bracket the physical loss of one O.5.2 leaf and 187 lines after editorial line 4619. The missing lines are not included in the 6,995 total and are not reconstructed numerically. Wright summarized the missing episode from the other English version in his omitted preface rather than inserting that prose into the poem.

The checked-in XML retains Wright's title-page matter, the complete extant romance on printed pp. 1–223, and 161 prose entries from the comparison appendix on pp. 224–225. It omits the verbal contents of the preface and glossary/index while retaining their page-break scaffolding. The encoded comparison entries reproduce readings from printed fragments mediated through Furnivall's preface and postscript; they are not additional stanzas of the cataloged work.

extent.description: One cataloged verse work, represented together with two zero-item editorial/carrier parts: the retained source title-page matter and Wright's comparison appendix. The romance is 6,995 extant L elements in 1,000 LG containers: 998 seven-line groups, one six-line group at lines 4614–4619, and one three-line group at lines 4620–4622. A missing O.5.2 leaf between present ff. 24v and 25r caused a 187-line loss after line 4619; missing text is excluded from the extant total. The XML also has 256 distinct PB elements, 74 folio milestones from 1a through 37b, 221 NOTE1 elements, and two BODY DIV1 containers. The second DIV1 has 161 P elements from printed pp. 224–225; those comparison entries and title-page prose are modeled but do not increment the intellectual-work item or form totals. Omitted preface and index divisions contain page placeholders rather than their verbal text.

content_parts.label: Wright source-edition title-page matter
content_parts.summary: The retained half-title/distributor/title/printer sequence identifies the 1878 completed EETS issue and numbers 55 and 70; its paper-manuscript and about-1440 wording is preserved as historical edition paratext, not accepted as current witness description.

locators.value: FRONT title-page sequence; PB SEQ 1–4, with TITLEPAGE beginning at SEQ 2
PDF pp. 10–13; the final title page is PDF p. 12

content_parts.label: Generydes (stanzaic version)
content_parts.summary: The complete extant rhyme-royal narrative, with a 187-line internal lacuna caused by one missing manuscript leaf.

locators.value: ff. 1r–37v; one leaf missing between present ff. 24v and 25r

notes: The principal witness is complete at its opening and ending but internally lacunose.
The 1,000 LG total is an encoding-container count; the two truncated groups around the missing leaf are not normalized into complete seven-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: Various readings from printed fragments
content_parts.summary: Wright's 161 encoded prose entries comparing printed-fragment readings reported in Furnivall's preface and postscript with the O.5.2 edition.

locators.value: preface and 1866 postscript fragment transcriptions, especially pp. xxv–xxx and xxxiii*–xxxvi*

notes: This apparatus does not contain 161 additional intellectual sections of the romance and does not make the medieval work prose or mixed-form.

related_works.title: Generides
notes: This broader romance-level identity encompasses the stanzaic and couplet English versions without erasing their distinct texts, forms, witnesses, and source claims.

related_works.title: Generides (couplet version)
notes: The 10,086-line rhyming-couplet text is an independent long version, not another witness of the exact stanzaic text. Wright used it only to summarize the episode lost with O.5.2's missing leaf.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2706.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; header, title page, BODY text and various-readings divisions
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for AHA2706 identifiers, 2006 uncorrected OCR/TEI Level 1 production, source bibliography, title-page matter, complete extant romance, 161 comparison entries, page-image references, and omitted preface/index boundaries.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped structural and lineation audit of the pinned aha2706 XML.
evidence.locator: HEADER, FRONT, BODY, and BACK census; all BODY DIV1, LG, L, P, PB, MILESTONE, and NOTE1 elements
evidence.summary: Found two BODY DIV1 containers, 1,000 LG, 6,995 L, 161 apparatus P, 256 distinct PB, 74 folio MILESTONE, and 221 NOTE1 elements. Exactly 998 LG have seven lines; the six-line and three-line groups bracket the stated 187-line loss after line 4619. Printed romance coverage is pp. 1–223 and comparison coverage pp. 224–225.

evidence.citation: William Aldis Wright, ed., Generydes: A Romance in Seven-Line Stanzas, Early English Text Society, Original Series 55 and 70 (London: N. Trübner, 1873–1878).
evidence.locator: 1873 Part I wrapper; title page and preface pp. v–x; romance pp. 1–223 = PDF pp. 20–242; various readings pp. 224–225 = PDF pp. 243–244
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the two-part issue, O.5.2 as Wright's continuous source, the missing leaf and 187-line lacuna after line 4619, authorship/source uncertainty, editorial method, prose summary from the other version, comparison readings mediated through Furnivall, and exact printed boundaries.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AHA2706.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current official item page for the encoded romance, lineation, page links, bibliographic identity, stable identifier, and U-M public-domain/No Copyright - United States assertion.

evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the O.5.2 shelfmark, fifteenth-century parchment composite, 211-folio extent, Generydes locus and incipit/explicit, adjacent works, provenance, physical description, and complete IIIF route.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge complete IIIF facsimile and audited local bundle for MS O.5.2.
evidence.locator: 430 canvases total; Generydes canvases 5–78 / ff. 1r–37v; missing leaf between canvas 52 f. 24v and canvas 53 f. 25r
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms parchment support, decorated double-column layout, exact opening and ending surfaces, the physical break between present ff. 24v and 25r, and whole-codex boundaries. Inventory, per-member SHA-256, JPEG decode, ZIP integrity, and direct-I/O whole-file hash audits passed.

evidence.citation: University of York, Database of Middle English Romance, 'Generides'.
evidence.locator: date, place, form, synopsis, manuscripts, early modern prints, and modern editions
evidence.summary: Current version-level evidence for late-fourteenth-century composition, Midlands localization, two distinct English versions, rhyme-royal form, O.5.2 c. 1470, Emmanuel 405 fragments, three early printed editions, Wright's EETS edition, Stubbings's fragment edition, and narrative subjects.
notes: The site's detailed plot summary identifies Furnivall's couplet edition as its summary source; this record uses it only for broad shared narrative orientation and verifies stanzaic claims against the target XML.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 2557.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-2557 and witnesses wit-2557-1 through wit-2557-4; data/Manuscripts.xml CamTCC1283 and CamEmm405
evidence.summary: Versioned census evidence for DIMEV 2557 / IMEV-NIMEV 1515, title Generydes, 6,995 rhyme-royal lines, O.5.2 ff. 1ra–37vb with one missing leaf, nine damaged Emmanuel folios, early-print fragments, and Wright and Stubbings editions. Record 111 separately identifies the 10,086-line couplet version.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is a prerelease; census claims are pinned to this exact release.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Royal Historie of the Excellent Knight Generides (Hertford, 1865; postscript dated 19 February 1866).
evidence.locator: preface pp. xxv–xxx and postscript pp. xxxiii*–xxxvi*; complete bound scan
evidence.summary: The immediate printed intermediary named by Wright's comparison appendix. Its main continuous text is the distinct couplet version; its preface and 1866 postscript reproduce early-print fragments of the stanzaic tradition and document Wright's rediscovery of O.5.2.

open_questions.question: What demonstrable source relationship connects the stanzaic and couplet English versions, and what source lies behind the stanzaic poem?
next_steps: Consult current textual scholarship comparing both complete English versions and the Emmanuel and early-print fragments.
Do not transfer the couplet prologue's French-to-Latin-at-Hertford claim to the stanzaic version without version-specific evidence.
Keep any lost physical exemplar distinct from surviving witnesses and later comparison editions.

open_questions.question: How do Wright's Fenn and Trinity-binding fragment groups map object by object onto the modern c. 1504 Pynson and c. 1506/c. 1518 de Worde print census?
next_steps: Consult Stubbings 1993 and current ESTC/repository records for exact fragment shelfmarks, leaf identities, and printer assignments.
Preserve Wright's comparison through Furnivall until direct object-level use is established.
Do not merge fragment holdings or infer a direct Wright-to-fragment collation edge from modern identifications.

open_questions.question: Can the differing historical and current descriptions of O.5.2's material, date, hands, and uniqueness be reconciled more precisely?
next_steps: Prefer current Trinity parchment cataloging and the modern c. 1470 romance-database date for discovery.
Retain Wright's vellum/mid-fifteenth-century preface observation and his contradictory paper/about-1440 title wording as attributed history.
Treat O.5.2 as the principal complete witness while acknowledging the later-discovered Emmanuel fragment.

notes: The medieval work, each physical witness or print state, Wright's two issue parts, later bound scans, U-M encoding, repository artifact, catalogs, and image bundles are separate descriptive layers.
O.5.2 is the principal complete witness, not the only surviving material for the stanzaic version.
The 6,995-line and 1,000-LG counts are reproducible encoding metrics; the 187 missing lines remain separate from extant extent.

Rights for medieval text, Wright and Furnivall editions, U-M encoding, repository XML, Internet Archive/Google scans, DIMEV data, and Trinity images are recorded separately in the linked lineage manifest.
